openingMessage: You don't rise to the level of your expectations. You fall to the level of your preparation. Come ready.
leadershipThought: Observation → Impact → Request. That is the structure. Use it before the job starts, not after something goes wrong.
opsContent:
Active Jobs (2026-06-22):
• Stefanie Kirschke: AOL Texas Ten Oneal 39Q 218H (5:53 AM) → back to shop (8:33 AM).
• Michael Jobe: AOL, waiting on crew to arrive (6:39 AM).
• Ray Solis: Arrived on location (6:47 AM). Potential scope add — disconnect at University flagged, awaiting confirmation (8:43 AM).
• Alex Clubb: Arrived on the Christi (6:49 AM).
• Dermont Morgan: Arrived Helwig 3TM (6:49 AM) → Ray Reed 32 114H complete (12:56 PM).
• Caleb Smith: Arrived Helwig 3TM (6:55 AM). Ray Reed 32 114H — permit live, starting disconnect (12:37 PM).
• Nathan Contreras: Done at King D, headed to next location (8:36 AM) → arrived Tracy B #1 (8:53 AM).
• Ralph Forbes: Arrived SM Odin rig 632 rig-down (8:22 AM) → rigged down, back to shop (9:49 AM).
• Dennis Easley: SM Energy Gipple 124H — 10,000 ppm H2S (2:18 PM). Third consecutive day of elevated readings.
• Michael Hutt: SM Coonskin 1500 hr pull — 3,000 ppm H2S (2:49 PM). Still unresolved.
• Andrew Bailey: Finished on Probandt 58.
• Dawson Drake: One separator fully cleaned. Finishing rods on second separator Tuesday (6:02 PM).

Carry-Forward into Tuesday June 23:
• [ ] Gipple 124H (Dennis Easley) — 10,000 ppm H2S three days running, confirm escalation and monitoring plan
• [ ] SM Coonskin #152 (Michael Hutt) — 3,000 ppm H2S still unresolved, confirm before next visit
• [ ] Schlitz A second separator (Dawson Drake) — rod work resumes Tuesday
• [ ] University disconnect (Ray Solis) — scope add flagged, confirm job status
lessonsLearned:
• Gipple 124H — 10,000 ppm H2S, three days running (Dennis Easley): Third consecutive day of extremely elevated atmospheric readings at this location. No resolution confirmed. Needs clear escalation plan and atmospheric monitoring protocol before any crew returns.
• SM Coonskin 1500 hr pull — 3,000 ppm H2S (Michael Hutt): Multiple days of elevated readings with no confirmed resolution. Confirm status with leadership before next crew visit.
• Scope change mid-day — University disconnect added (Ray Solis): Job scope potentially expanded mid-shift. Communicated proactively — good discipline. Confirm plan before mobilizing.
exxonMoments:
Kill the Well First — Pressure Verification Is Not a Formality (Breaking Containment)
Crew managed active pressure while killing a well and lifting a polished rod. Atmospheric monitoring and respiratory protection in place throughout. Confirm the well is killed and on vacuum before breaking any connections — that verification step is not paperwork, it is the last barrier.

Multi-Trade Tailgate Before First Break (Breaking Containment)
Before a disconnect in Midkiff, the Canon tech ran a tailgate with electricians, roustabouts, and MTs — all trades, together, before the first break. Exclusion zones defined, atmospheric hazards reviewed. Every trade on location needs to know the exclusion zone before containment is broken — not just Canon.

Verified Zero Pressure Before Breaking Containment on the T (Breaking Containment)
When containment was broken on the T-connection, crew had already confirmed no pressure between the break points. Spills were mitigated and the area was left clean. Zero pressure verification is the checkpoint that separates a controlled break from an uncontrolled release.

LEL Detected — Roustabouts Switched to Hand Tools (Atmospheric Monitoring)
LEL was detected from the well before work started. Roustabout crew immediately switched to hand tools only — no power tools in an explosive atmosphere. Simple decision, correctly made. When the monitor speaks, the tools change.

Bleed Off Every Valve Before You Start Turning Wrenches (Breaking Containment)
Job at Robbie 17-8 went well but crew identified one improvement: make sure all valves are fully bled off before breaking connections. A bull plug was later replaced with a tapped bull plug at TXL 36 for the same reason — bleed-off points need to be accessible and confirmed zero before any line is broken.

fieldNews.headline: Ralph Forbes — SM Odin rig 632 rigged down clean
fieldNews.detail: In by 8:22 AM, rigged down and back to shop by 9:49 AM. Clean, efficient turnaround with no issues reported.

fieldNews.headline: Caleb Smith + Dermont Morgan — Ray Reed 32 114H disconnect complete
fieldNews.detail: Caleb confirmed permit live before starting and gave a proactive update at 12:37 PM. Dermont confirmed job complete at 12:56 PM. Good communication and clean execution.

fieldNews.headline: Gipple 124H — 10,000 ppm H2S, third consecutive day (Dennis Easley)
fieldNews.detail: Readings: 11,000 ppm on 06-18, 10,000 ppm today. No resolution confirmed. Escalation plan and atmospheric monitoring protocol needed before next crew visit.

fieldNews.headline: SM Coonskin 1500 hr pull — 3,000 ppm H2S (Michael Hutt)
fieldNews.detail: Multiple days of elevated readings with no confirmed resolution. Confirm status with leadership before next crew visit.

fieldNews.headline: Scope change mid-day — University disconnect (Ray Solis)
fieldNews.detail: Potential job scope add flagged at 8:43 AM. Communicated proactively — good discipline. Confirm plan and permit requirements before mobilizing.

todaysChallenge: Before you speak on the next job — ask yourself: What outcome am I trying to create?
todaysChallengeSubtext: If you can't answer that, don't speak yet. Outcome-driven communication solves problems. Emotion-driven communication creates them.

compliance.footerNote: Update numbers in daily.json each Monday morning.
